artifacts.content:
Hi Danny,

Finally sitting down after getting Sofia through her math homework and Lucas into the shower. I wanted to write everything out from today before it blurs together.

Sofia had another rough day at Riverside. She had a full panic attack in the cafeteria this afternoon when the 6th-grade team threw an unplanned celebration with cupcakes and soda again, even though we’ve talked a million times about the new guidelines. It was basically a replay of March 4, 2024, when she melted down in the cafeteria because the class party turned into cupcakes and soda against the rules. I ended up walking her to Lauren Kim’s office, and we did breathing exercises until she calmed down.

I emailed her pediatric nutritionist at Hershey Medical Center tonight to let them know what happened and to ask if they have any updated recommendations. Between that and the May 2024 endocrinology appointment at Penn State Health Milton S. Hershey Medical Center—when they first pushed us to eliminate soda and juice from classroom celebrations—I feel like all I do is fight the same battle from different angles.

The only silver lining is that it gives me more leverage with the Wellness Committee when we meet again in April to tighten the classroom celebration procedures and retrain everyone. It’s one thing to talk about “policy,” it’s another to watch your own kid shake in the cafeteria.

Anyway, tomorrow is the usual Tuesday chaos: Sofia’s band practice at Riverside, Lucas’s soccer behind Riverview Elementary. Can you grab Lucas if my after-school meeting runs long? I’ll try to get home in time to throw together chicken tacos by 6:30.

Love you,
Maria

Personal reflection – 2024-10-20

I can’t stop replaying the Hispanic Heritage Month mess with Ms. Alvarez. During the October 2024 celebration in her 7th-grade classroom at Riverside, I had to quietly shut down her handing out homemade churros and cans of regular Coca-Cola she’d picked up on sale at the Giant on Main Street. They violated both Smart Snacks and our free-beverage guidelines, and kids were already cracking them open when I walked in.

In the moment, I defaulted to “policy voice” and reminded her about the ban on sugary beverages being offered for free during the school day. Later, on 2024-10-18, she came to my office and told me she’d used her own money even though she’s still paying off medical bills from her gallbladder surgery at UPMC Harrisburg. She looked so embarrassed. I felt like I’d humiliated her in front of her homeroom, even though I tried to be discreet.

This is the same tension I felt back in August 2023 when we removed the Pepsi-branded scoreboard wrap in the Riverside Middle School gym. Mark Donnelly, our athletic booster president, was furious in that 2023-08-22 main office meeting, talking about the 2021 side agreement—$2,500 per year in cash plus free soda for the concession stand, all funneled through the Riverview Community Credit Union. He basically admitted that if the district business office ever audited the booster account, they’d “have questions.”

Need to find a better way to support people through these changes so it doesn’t feel like I’m policing them or exposing their financial stress, while still protecting kids’ health and keeping us in compliance.

metadata.title: Balancing wellness enforcement with staff realities

artifacts.content:
Hi Mami,

Just wanted to catch you up on Lucas and say thank you again.

At his last checkup at Riverview Family Health, his pediatrician was worried about how fast he’s gaining weight and mentioned his blood pressure is borderline high again. Looking back at his 2023–2024 well-child visits, the growth chart really tells the story. It scared me enough that at our 1/14/25 Wellness Committee meeting in the conference room by the main office at Riverside, I used Lucas’s chart (without his name, of course) as an example when I pushed everyone to tighten the portion sizes in our snack guidelines and finally remove the last full-sugar sports drinks from the after-school intramural program.

I know it’s personal, but I keep thinking if my own son is struggling, there must be so many other kids in the same boat.

Also, thank you for letting me use your SNAP card and senior discount at the Riverview Giant supermarket back in September to buy the fruit, string cheese, and whole-grain crackers. We spent about $186 to stock a healthier snack table for three home basketball games at the Riverside gym. The kids actually bought the fruit! The athletic booster treasurer was thrilled we didn’t blow the budget, but I still feel weird that we had to lean on your benefits to make it happen.

Are you still free this Thursday to come over for arroz con pollo? Sofia wants to show you the song she’s learning in band, and Lucas is determined to beat you at Uno again.

Te quiero mucho,
Maria

metadata.subject: Lucas, the games, and thank you

artifacts.content:
Policy drafting thoughts – 2024-03-12

I spent half of today thinking about that conversation with Ryan Blake last week. The 7th grader he’s been seeing who started hoarding candy from classroom incentives and binge eating in the bathroom near Room 214 at Riverside is exactly why we can’t keep pretending food-based rewards are harmless.

In March 2024 I added the clause discouraging food-based rewards into the nutrition guidelines draft, but I need to make sure the language is strong enough to actually shift practice. Ryan and I agreed we should push teachers toward nonfood rewards like extra recess, art time, or positive calls home. I sent him an email this afternoon saying I’d reference the incident in my internal notes but not in anything public-facing.

What makes this even more urgent is what happened on May 9, 2024 with the 6th-grade class: a teacher handed out full-size candy bars for PSSA test prep, and the student with the binge-eating disorder history and inpatient treatment at Belmont Behavioral Hospital ate four bars and ended up vomiting in my office. I spent an hour on the phone with the parent and the counselor afterward.

Between the bathroom hoarding near Room 214 and the May 9 vomiting incident, I don’t need more evidence. I’ve already blocked off August 2024 on the calendar for mandatory nonfood-reward training for all staff. Now I just have to design PD that doesn’t feel like a scolding but still makes it clear that candy as a classroom management tool is off the table.

Hi Lauren,

Wanted to put a few things in writing while they’re fresh, partly so we can think about next steps together.

First, J.’s mom pulled me aside at conferences last week (November 2024) and told me that since he started the doctor-prescribed meal plan at Riverview Family Health Clinic back in October 2023, he’s down 18 pounds over the 2023–2024 school year. She said a big part of it was him switching from daily chips and soda to eating school lunch and choosing Smart Snacks from the machines instead. It’s one kid, but it feels like tangible evidence that all the menu changes, vending clean-up, and fundraiser rules are actually helping.

On the staff side, I’ve been thinking about M. in 8th-grade math. After his mild heart attack and hospitalization at UPMC Pinnacle Harrisburg in February 2024, he told me in November that the fall 2024 step challenge and the healthier snacks we’ve been putting in the Riverside staff lounge helped him get his blood pressure down enough that his cardiologist reduced his meds. Again, anecdotal, but it’s hard not to see the connection.

And then there’s Sofia. Between her panic attack in the Riverside cafeteria on March 4, 2024 during that cupcake-and-soda “surprise” party and the follow-up we did with her pediatric nutritionist at Hershey Medical Center, I feel like my family is the walking case study for why this work matters.

Maybe we can brainstorm a way to capture stories like these for our own internal motivation, even if we never share names or details beyond our little wellness circle.

Thanks for everything you do,
Maria

metadata.subject: Anecdotal wins from students, staff, and home

artifacts.content:
Javier: How’s the wellness crusade going, sis? You sounded wiped last time.

Maria: Still wiped. 🙃 This week was a greatest-hits reel of all my second-guessing.

Maria: Remember that Pepsi-branded scoreboard wrap we took down in the Riverside gym back in August 2023? Mark Donnelly is STILL salty. In that 8/22/23 meeting in the main office he told me about the 2021 side deal—$2,500 a year plus free soda for the concession stand, all tucked into the Riverview Community Credit Union booster account—and basically hinted an audit would raise eyebrows.

Javier: Yikes. That’s…not subtle.

Maria: Exactly. Fast forward to this fall and I’m at the Riverview Giant with Mami, using her SNAP card and senior discount to buy $186 worth of fruit, string cheese, and whole-grain crackers so we can pilot a healthier concession table for three home basketball games without blowing the booster budget. Feels like I’m patching holes in a boat someone else drilled.

Javier: You always end up fixing things you didn’t break.

Maria: Same story with the classroom side. The March 4, 2024 cupcake-and-soda fiasco that sent Sofia into a panic attack in the Riverside cafeteria is why I keep tightening the celebration procedures. Between that and her May 2024 endocrinology visit at Hershey, I’m done pretending “just one party” is harmless.

Maria: Anyway, enough venting. How’s Pittsburgh? Still thinking about moving closer to Riverview or just flirting with the idea? We’re doing library + park runs this weekend if you decide to surprise us.

Event: Wellness Committee Working Session – Celebration Procedures & Rewards
Date: 2024-08-15
Time: 2:00–4:00 p.m.
Location: Riverside Middle School – conference room by main office
Attendees: Maria Hernandez, Lauren Kim (nurse), Ryan Blake (counselor), James O’Malley (food service), two teacher reps, PTO rep, student rep TBD

Agenda / Notes for myself:

1. Start with a quick recap of why we’re here:
   - March 4, 2024: Sofia’s panic attack in the Riverside cafeteria when cupcakes and soda showed up at an “unplanned” 6th-grade celebration, despite the new guidelines.
   - May 9, 2024: 6th-grade teacher handed out full-size candy bars for PSSA prep; student with binge-eating disorder and prior inpatient stay at Belmont Behavioral Hospital ate four bars and vomited in my office.
   - Ongoing concerns from Ryan about the 7th grader hoarding candy and binge eating in the bathroom near Room 214.

2. Celebration procedures:
   - Revisit the no soda/juice rule that grew out of Sofia’s May 2024 endocrinology appointment at Penn State Health Milton S. Hershey Medical Center and conversations with her pediatric nutritionist at Hershey Medical Center.
   - Clarify approval process so there are no more “surprise” parties with noncompliant food.

3. Rewards:
   - Finalize language discouraging food-based rewards; build in concrete nonfood options (extra recess, art time, positive calls home).
   - Plan the mandatory nonfood-reward PD for all staff in late August 2024.

4. Next steps:
   - Draft parent-friendly summary.
   - Schedule follow-up check-in for October.

Bring: copies of draft language, incident notes (internal only), sample nonfood reward list.
